comments.title: Seeking Stillness and Meditating on Poor Scrying
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Where I am, we've had some egregiously beautiful weather the last couple of days, and look to get a couple more before things become merely "fine." Wherever you are, I hope you've weathered the eclipse successfully and have some similar pleasant local conditions.

To Share: A shorter post this week on a couple of ways that I look for stillness in a much-agitated world: https://jpowellrussell.com/#seeking_stillness

To Ask: I've noticed over the last few months that sometimes when a scrying goes "poorly," I can still get rather a lot out of the meditations about the scrying. Other than "yup, that's one of the reasons you do the follow-up meditations," do you have any thoughts on this seeming oddity of practice?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings and best wishes to all who will have them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Seeking Stillness and Meditating on Poor Scrying
replies.content:
Yup, that's one of the reasons you do the follow-up meditations. ;-)

The reason is that your subjective impression of the quality of a scrying need not have much to do with how well you connected to the theme of the scrying; a lot of extraneous factors can get in the way. To use some old-fashioned occult jargon, your subjective mind (aka subconscious) can absorb much more than your objective mind (aka consciousness) can process, and meditation is one of the ways you can tap into the latent perceptions of the subjective mind.

replies.title: Re: Seeking Stillness and Meditating on Poor Scrying
replies.content: Thank you Jeff for what you have shared here and over on JMG's other blog. Very, very helpful to me. Thank you also for the blessings and I hope you will accept my reciprocal one.

replies.title: Re: Seeking Stillness and Meditating on Poor Scrying
replies.content:
You're quite welcome, and I'm so glad to hear it has helped! Thank you as well for your blessings.

Jeff

comments.title: Prayer List ***AUTUMN SPECIAL***
comments.content:
If we work at it, each of us surely has at least one thing about ourselves that we feel sure we can improve, but we haven’t managed it yet. Something that would improve our lives, that would improve us. I’d like to invite everyone reading this post to take a few moments of reflection to see if they can identify such a desired change in themselves. To those that can, I’d like to offer you the chance to take the plunge and attempt to make that change with some aid from the prayers of members of this community.

There is only one price of admission: whatever you request, you must take concrete actions in the world to increase your chances or rates of success there, for the duration of however long your request stays on the list. Prayers can act as the wind in your sails, but they do little good if you don’t even raise your sails in the first place! Or worse, cast anchor and sit there… of even start rowing your boat in the opposite way…

Besides that one requirement, normal Prayer List FAQ rules apply: requests can be pseudonymous; it’s nice to get regular updates from folks; and I hope people receiving prayers will consider praying for at least a few others on the prayer list in some semi-regular fashion, at least for as long as they are on the list. (None of these things are required, though I think it’s nice to do.)

Blessings on all who will have them, and I hope some will join me in this endeavor! I’ll certainly be adding a prayer request along these lines of my own in the near future. Perhaps I’ll see you soon.

comments.content:
I'm in the process of writing a novel where a plot point is that a means is discovered to avoid radiation poisoning, prompting a trip to Mars, and the people sent on this trip slowly get sick and die; despite the fact that there's nothing physically wrong with them, all the tests show them to be in perfect health, they slowly get weaker and then end up dying.

I've realized that this sounds a lot like etheric starvation, which seems like a real risk with long distance space travel. The ISS is supplied with food regularly, has regular turnover, and is close enough to Earth that it might still be within the etheric bounds of our world in a way a spacecraft heading to Mars isn't.

1) Do you know any sources I could look at for what it looks like when someone's etheric body is starved, in case it suggests other details for the story?

2) Is it possible that etheric starvation plays a role in the way astronauts are so often extremely weak when they return from trips to orbit? This would be in addition, of course, to the effects of zero gravity, radiation, and other factors.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, what's your explanation for their sickness and death within the novel? In real life you can get away with something just happening for no reason, but fiction has to be more logical than that. If you're going to make it a matter of etheric starvation, of course, you'll need to make sure that by the time this comes out, the reader knows plenty about the ether and the etheric body.

1) Read up on vampirism. Victims of vampires are good examples of what etheric depletion looks like.

2) That's an intriguing hypothesis, though I don't imagine it'll be tested any time too soon!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sorry to butt in here, but in response to (2), I think there has been some indirect testing. NASA has done quite a lot of bedrest testing to calibrate their human experiments on ISS, and to my understanding there's no real discrepancy-- muscle wastage in orbit is about what you'd expect based on measured activity level. (Those human experiments are really the main reason that boondoggle has ever had to exist, after Shuttle-Mir showed Space Industrialization was never going to pay-- to throw some guinea pigs up into orbit for months or years and claim it was a 'stepping-stone to mars'. Which is rubbish, because serious mission proposals tumble or spin the ship so the astronauts weren't too weak to do real exploring when they got to the surface. ISS should be testing long-term effects of that motion on the inner ear, but they won't because it's a subsidy dumpster, not a serious enterprise. Even Skylab did better in that respect, since the Astronauts did test jogging around the walls 2001-style.)

Also, it doesn't take sci-fi magic to get to Mars without radiation poisoning-- you just need to wrap your astronaut's habitat in several meters of water. Does it take sci-fi magic to get all those many, many tonnes of water into orbit, along with enough fuel to move them, without bankrupting the entire western world? Why... yes, yes it does.

All that said, I like OP's etheric starvation idea. Since radionics seems to hint at a link between the electromagnetic and the etheric, and ISS is well within the Earth's geomagnetic field, you can easily justify in the narrative the idea that ISS is within Earth's etheric body (or whatever it is that planets have).

--Tyler A.

comments.content:
Hello ArchDruid

I am having a bit of a crisis of faith. Like all of us here (probably all of us) I have the pleasure/unpleasure of living the world of western civilization (Europe to be exact).


I've been practicing magic for 2-3 years, mostly without some sort of ongoing training. I undertook such only about a year ago.
I've generally dabbled in natural magic, sat a bit in witchcraft practices based on Gemma's books or Chumbley's traditions or just folk practices, without bragging with pretty good results.

However, I have a problem. It is known, working full time, living with other people, just a normal life, trivial you could say. Recently, I have been wondering about the sense of magic practice (I do not want to abandon it, Gods forbid, I love magic with all my being). But...I can cure someone's illness with a spell, that is, I have achieved the power of...paracetamol. I can talk to spirits but so what if it mostly has no physical direct effects. Magic is very subtle which makes me wonder what the end goal is? What can be done using it that a modern person would call "practical". As much as I love spiritual pondering and learning about the nature of the world, these practices will not put bread on my family's table and there are only so many hours in the day.

So tl;dr I wonder what are the real possibilities of magic to influence the world, maybe what cultural sources best show them? Without magic like from Harry Potter but what is real.

replies.title: Practical magic
replies.content:
Hi,
I have been practicing magic for 9 years now and have mostly worked on improving myself, cleaning off negative characteristics and adding good characteristics. My family is very pleased with the results.

I have also done some magic to increase my pleasure and success in painting and that worked very well. I have used magic to help me learn to play a musical instrument.

I dare say, you could use magic to earn more money by honest means and you can use magic to help yourself become more frugal. For that sort of magic, I recommend reading a copy of, "Your Money or Your Life," by Jo Dominguez and Vicky Robinson.

Being a happier, more grateful, more resilient person is something I think magic may be able to help you with.
Maxine

replies.content: Hi Maxine, off topic and I'm not even sure I'll reach you now-- but I'm trying to track you down to make sure you saw my comment last month about your sister. I really felt the moment meaningful in retrospect. Whatever she has been through, I think she is doing fine now and likely has been so for some time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is a common question, and it has a very simple answer. If you want to do practical things in the material world, you're better off picking up practical skills relevant to the material world. If you want to become rich, for example, become a stockbroker, not a mage!

Magic is an art; like most arts, it's very demanding and the payoff in strictly material terms is generally not very high. The effects of magic are subtle, and they're at their strongest when used to change yourself; you can become a wiser, stronger, happier, and more successful person using magic -- but you have to approach it with that in mind, and with a willingness to let go both of unhelpful habits and of the notion that you should change the world instead of changing yourself.

comments.title: Ethical love spells
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

I am in a place in my life such that I would like to find a compatible partner with whom I can spend the rest of my life.

Many love spells in ancient grimoires seem more like spiritual rape spells or domination aimed at particular people. Since I do not want to engage in the former, and have no particular person in mind, what would be the best magical approach to find love?

I recall reading in the past that you have suggested making oneself more lovable as an adjunct to love spells. In a practical magical sense, what would a spell of this sort look like?

(I am most familiar with the Golden Dawn tradition from your books, if it helps.)

P

replies.title: Re: Ethical love spells
replies.content: Making yourself more lovable isn't an adjunct to love spells, it's the only kind of love spell that works reliably without blowback. If you know your Golden Dawn material well, a Netzach working is a very good approach. Study the symbolism and lore relating to Netzach, the seventh Sphere of the Tree; draw up a talisman ritual for a talisman of Netzach to make you a lovable person; make the talisman and consecrate it on the day and hour of Venus; then put it aside, try not to think about it, and let it do its work. I warn you that most such workings start by showing you all the unlovable aspects of yourself, but that's how you figure out what you need to change; with the steady gentle pressure of the talisman behind you, the changes will be easier than you expect, and love will come your way -- to judge by examples I've seen in practice, sooner than you think.

replies.title: Re: Metal Music
replies.content:
I'll share with you my own personal experiences. I've been a huge metal fan since high school (30+ years at this point). I grew up in a Christian home, and my dad kept a close eye on the specific music choices I made. Most things were allowed, but not anything with explicitly evil content.

I maintained this "rule" for myself for years afterwards. About 10 years ago, for many different reasons, I started listening to bands that I normally wouldn't have (Ghost, Lucifer, Sabbath Assembly, etc.). Certainly, I had developed a mental state beforehand that led me down that path. But, adding in that music created a feedback loop that lasted several years and made me unrecognizable to myself - negativity, nihilism, selfishness, laziness, feeling like a powerless victim, etc.

Fortunately, 3.5 years ago, I snapped out of it. I still listen to metal, but that evil stuff is completely uninteresting to me. Beyond that, a lot of stuff that isn't explicitly evil but just dark (Opeth, Swallow the Sun, Uada) has very little time in my playlists these days. I spend most of my time with more positive influences (Iron Maiden, Dream Theater, Stryper, Theocracy). Again, I'm not saying the music is causal, but it generates feedback loops that I've seen firsthand and want to use for positive not destruction.

Also, on that same note, I had my heart broken in high school and had developed a sort of mental playlist of songs during that time. Over the years, I would revisit those songs with sadness and nostalgia. I have since banned those songs as well, and it has helped me tremendously.

Good Morning JMG and the Commentariat!

Thank you for providing this space for questions. I'm going to steal jprussell's comment format today. I hope he doesn't mind.

To Share: Last week there was an anonymous comment discussing Joseph Smith's involvement in magic and the occult. JMG replied, "I've hoped for years that someday one of the LDS churches -- there are a number of them, as you may know -- will rise to the challenge of the movement's origins and become the magical religion that Joseph Smith arguably founded."

I was doing some research on priesthood robes for a completely unrelated topic and came across this website: https://cjccf.org The Church of Jesus Christ in Christian Fellowship, which is another Mormonism based online group. comments.title: Land acknowledgements
comments.content:
Recently, I was reading several new books from the outhouse moon people. Their authors have land acknowledgements in the front - such as we recognized that this land was taken from this group of Native people, blah, blah.

Anyway, this has been creeping into general Neo-Pagan rituals of acknowledging (stolen) land. Does this affect the magic in anyway? Also, when doing Ancestor work, and working with the Ancestors of the Land, how does one recognize the prior inhabitants? Or do they?

My own opinion is that at least in the Northeast, that everyone fought everyone else, to the point of one group won. Like the way the Romans or Mongols conquered other lands. Moreover, I don't see in these land acknowledgements, any movement to repay the people or to give back the land.

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content:
Yes, I read once about a grim joke made by a contemporary Wampanoag: “Thank goodness for the English, or we’d be speaking Narragansett now.” The area around Providence, RI was heavily contested ground among native groups, and at one point the Wampanoag allied with the English against the Narragansett to try to push them back.

I find the land acknowledgment trend so depressingly simple minded and ignorant. It reduces native people to one-dimensional, weak victims who never had any strategy, agency, or complexity in their dealings with each other and European groups. Completely fake and racist.

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content: I've been watching the whole "land acknowledgment" business with a raised eyebrow for some time. Since the people who do it aren't giving back the land or doing anything else to address the matter, aren't they simply rubbing the noses of the native peoples in the theft?  Something along the lines of "We won, you lost, nyah nyah nyah," but with a facade of fake politeness?

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content:
There's something very, very weird about it. I live in Canada, where it has caught on in a very real way, and the people doing it genuinely seem to think it does something. I grew up in a managerial class, and so I've picked up on the art of recognizing when there's some ulterior motive; and here, I haven't picked up the faintest whiff of it.

I haven't been able to make any sense of it, but I'm confident that whatever it is is weirder than it seems to be; and it already seems pretty weird...

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content:
That's interesting -- and spooky. I wonder what they think it's doing...

...and whether they might be saying, in effect, "and we'll be gone soon, so you can have it back."

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content:
Not that anybody is going to do anything about it, as you say, but the whole idea raises the question of how far back we should go. If we give land back to the native Americans, can I have my ancestors' land back from whoever is living there now?

Take the successive invasions of the British isles for example. The Romans came, then the Angles and Saxons, then the Vikings, then the Normans. And we know nothing of whatever waves of migration there might have been before that.

I don't know much detail about the pre-Columbian political history of North America, but I would guess that the distribution of land among various groups was rarely static for very long. Native American tribes did not just exist in a steady state from the beginning of the world until 1492. Or after 1492, for that matter.

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content: In Australia it is similarly getting weirder. I could get on board in the early days with the idea of opening events with a welcome to country, or starting a meeting acknowledging the traditional owners, if it meant increasing awareness of who the local traditional people are and creating real life interactions. But mostly it’s become a canned generic statement that is intoned without any feeling or connection. And the “Always was, always will be (Aboriginal land)” repeated everywhere like a mantra is starting to give off a creepy vibe, maybe like a horde of growling zombies making its way toward me.

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content:
I’m exposed to a lot of events where those statements are made, and felt the same way. I got in touch with the Indian Land Tenure Foundation and after talking with them decided to make monthly donations to their Native Land Acquisition Fund and their Ag Extension Cooperative. This will help me not grind my teeth when listening to the land acknowledgements! On the other hand, quite honestly I might not have thought about getting in touch with the ITLF if I hadn’t been saturated with all those acknowledgements, so maybe it works……like magic!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content:
Funny you should mention it, as I've also been puzzling over this.

I've encountered the land-acknowledgement thing in PMC work circles, and find it a bit weird.

On one hand, one could dismiss it as just the usual vacuous virtue signalling, along with all the things that go with it, like pointedly calling Columbus Day "Indigenous People's Day," putting one's pronouns in one's email signature, keeping up with the "euphemism treadmill" (the latest seems to be using "enslaved persons" instead of "slaves" and all the trans language like "birthing person" instead of woman, etc.). None of which have the slightest impact on actually making anything materially better for anyone, but which are stridently embraced by the PMC set - presumably because they're easy ways to feel like one is a "good person" without having to actually do anything meaningful at all, while also signalling to all and sundry who is in the "good" club vs. the "bad" club.

On the other hand, there does seem to be something a little extra-weird about the land acknowledgement routine, insofar that it's so specifically "this and not that," if you know what I mean. Why land, specifically? Why no "acknowledgements" of any things else, like, I dunno the exploited labor that made your clothes and computer, or that mined the lithium for your electric car battery? Why no acknowledgement even of more "politically correct" causes like, I dunno, the men who died fighting for the North in the Civil War, which conflict the PMC set believes whole-heatedly was entirely about slavery? There are any number of things they could virtue-signal about, so why LAND, in particular?

I've been wondering about this one for a while now. And yes, my wondering includes an occult dimension, which is why this comment is at least tangentially relevant here.

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content: Well, I think you answered your own question there. Acknowledging land theft admits to some long-ago sin that's over and done with; acknowledging the slave labor in your cell phone or the CO2 emitted to get you to your conference requires you to make some change. From my reading, this is one of the main, persistent gripes Native groups have in the US - everyone is willing to admit that the colonial days were exploitative, but no one remembers that the Indians are still here, often stuck on reservations with lousy support systems and in need of 21st century help. Lest I get too far off of occult topics, the land acknowledgement strikes me as very similar to the expression of sin at the beginning of Catholic mass - beating your chest and asking for mercy as the price of admission.

replies.title: Re: Land acknowledgements
replies.content:
How do you think they'd react to someone self-describing as birthingpersonerastic?

(The only illegitimate sexual category?)

replies.title: Re: Holy Water/Oil (Gnostic Lessons)

Then, our active energy depleted, our passivity often looks like straight up mindlessness- using tv or internet or something to avoid thinking entirely, and retreating from the ideals we loved but burned us out. replies.title: Healthy passivity
replies.content:
Hopefully JMG will have something more substantial to offer here…..but one approach I use a lot is to read meaningful and interesting, even inspiring novels as something I find both restful and refreshing after a fatiguing effort. I often reread old favorites and savor them slowly while I recover my energy for more active work. Keeping a shelf of old favorites is good—since you already know what they’re about then you’ll know what effect they’ll have on you and can choose what you need to retune your spirits!

I must add that all the novels in JMG’s Weird of Hali series are on my old favorites bookshelf!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The first thing to do, as you've noticed, is get control over your habit of overdoing the active phase. If you save energy in that part of the cycle, you'll have it to do more with the other half of the cycle, whereas if you go ahead and overdo, you don't have any choice but to collapse. Once you dial back the intensity of the busy cycle far enough, collapsing into passivity will bore you to tears, and you'll find other ways to be receptive.

(I stress this because in my experience, a lot of people in your situation secretly, or not so secretly, want to keep on overdoing it but don't want to have to put in the downtime. That doesn't work.)

Don't ever be passive, by the way. Be active when you choose, receptive when you choose, and when you want to rest, closing your eyes and letting the world go away while you nap will do you much more good than drowning your mind in the pseudo-rest provided by distraction technologies like TV and the internet. Keep in mind also that it's not just you -- the technologies just noted are designed to be addictive, and they're no better for you than binge drinking. You might consider getting rid of your TV -- as in literally selling it or throwing it out -- and making the internet less convenient for you; I know people who won't have internet access in the house and use the internet only when they visit the local public library, for example. (That's why I had Brecken Kendall in The Nyogtha Variations do that.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It may help to consider rest an active part of your training as opposed to something passive. Consider weight lifting - muscles are broken down while lifting. They don’t actually repair and grow until you’re resting. Rest with intention and schedule it into your routine.

Pro level athletes take off seasons and their on seasons will be scheduled intentionally with greater and lesser periods of activity.

When doing long distance running, the way to increase your distance is incrementally. You go up a couple kilometres, then back down up, then up a couple more, then back down again, until after weeks or months later you can run 15 Km, 21.1 Km, 42.20 km, whatever you’re training. No one is going from running 0 Km to 42.20 Km without any training without injuring themselves. This is the case with magic and spiritual or intellectual or other pursuits as well. Increase your work load gradually.

You may find it more productive to make a schedule for yourself, allowing for rest and recovery and your other activities, and have a plan for what you’ll be doing when and how you’re going to schedule more time consuming things into your daily life. I plan all my activities and workings and training plans in the same calendar so I can keep track. I treat spiritual work much in the same way as my physical training and plan to do certain things at certain times of the year, month, week, day, etc.

This may or may not be the case for you, but something I’ve noticed with the manic all or nothing attitude as well is a sense of perfectionism and control. It’s almost like if we’re not able to perfectly dedicate massive amounts of time to something that’s not our day job and spend 24/7 on it and be absolutely perfect and the best ever then somehow we’re not good enough and there’s no point doing it at all. We set unrealistic and unsustainable expectations for ourselves and then when we inevitably can’t meet them, we crash.

If this resonates for you, I would like to say that just because your practice isn’t perfect (whatever that may mean) doesn’t mean it’s not good. Make a plan. Schedule rest. Increase intensity gradually and with intention. Ask yourself if more training actually benefits your practice and brings you closer to your goals (physical or spiritual) or if you’ve added it in arbitrarily because you’ve just decided emotionally that you should do “more” for some reason. Adjust your plans as needed. Adapt to life because it will mess up your plans sometimes. Remember that just because you may not be able to do something 7 days a week, that doesn’t mean it’s not still good to do it 5 days a week. It’s okay to deviate from your training routine and come back to it later.

And if you fall off the horse, just climb back up! ♥️

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings everyone,

As a part of the kundalini rising going on with me right, I've been having a lot of emotional and mental breakthroughs in cycles, especially with regard to phenomenology/life, the nature of action and choice etc. I've been using divination to try and validate them (at the risk of the obvious confusion that emotional static can create).

One of the things I asked was if enlightenment/end of material incarnation was possible in a morally neutral way (just from say knowledge/understanding without necessarily involvement in human life or the suffering of others). It suggested no.

I also asked if karma was more of the psycho-emotional imprint generated by action between two or more individualities, that required that pattern to be reversed and undone in the future, and if it would be possible to do the same action without the same imprint/karma (something like skilful means of fulfilling desire)? It suggested there was some merit to this idea but I had to figure out more or conceptualise this differently. This would be akin to figuring out a phenomenology of hedonism within life.

So my question to JMG and everyone is, why is "good", or "light", not in a religious or moral sense, but in a deeper movement of our intentionality in the universe, in the nature of phenomena itself, embedded in the nature of the universe and of life? ie why is this the teleological direction for finishing up incarnation and why can't someone just spontaneously realise reality and combust out of it?

Is it something like that the realisation involves connection to reality itself and thus our connection to others?

(I haven't read the occult literature so I appreciate a condensed answer and pointers on what to read to learn more).

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Because your enlightenment is not just your own business. You are not separate from the cosmos; the higher you rise spiritually, the more you become aware of the fact that you are a manifestation of the Divine and your spiritual development is part of the process by which the Divine unfolds its own potentialities. The teleology of your soul is part of the teleology of the Divine, just as the process of growth of each individual cell in a fetus is part of the process by which a fertilized ovum becomes a new human being.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Thank you, this is helpful.

Do you have any thoughts on the karma generation / skilful means idea?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Karma isn't tit-for-tat; it's not a matter of "you stole an apple from me, so someday I will steal an apple from you." Karma expresses itself through character, and secondarily through circumstances. You can deal with it in three ways: you can suffer through it, by getting clobbered by the consequences of your actions; you can work through it, by grappling with the problems in your own character and changing yourself as needed; or you can grow through it, by reaching a level of awareness at which the problem solves itself. That last one sounds like the easiest, doesn't it?  It's not. It requires more effort and a greater willingness to suffer and work than either of the other two -- but it can be done.

John--

I have a deeply-rooted habit of measurement. Pertinent to my question here, this mental framework is constantly assessing my life as-it-is against the metric of my life as-it-could/should/might-be. This impacts my experience of life significantly, as you might imagine, and generally not for the better, as reality nearly always comes up short of the ideal. Some of these are/were excessive metrics (e.g. getting my PhD at the age of 21 versus the more typical age of 27 as I did) and some are more "normal" (e.g. not advancing as quickly at work as I'd like, or in the manner/form that I'd envisioned). One of the more relevant issues is the ideal I've long held in my mind re marriage: the vision of the passionate, fiery union of souls twining upwards to touch the Divine. My marriage is far more mundane and my wife often feels that I see it as "less than" -- something I am careful about voicing for obvious reasons. I understand that this ideal is not going to be met--just like I will never have been a seventeen year-old graduate student--but I've held these images for so long, they feel like permanent part of me and relinquishing them feels like settling. How can I most effectively go about ridding myself of these ideal visions so that I can better appreciate my life and wife?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I just happen to have an essay relevant to this topic. Hope this helps:

https://kimberlysteele.dreamwidth.org/83892.html

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, I'd recommend doing a lot of journaling to understand where it was that you picked up these wildly unrealistic notions of what life "should be." Typically those get absorbed in childhood, when you're too clueless to realize how damaging and self-defeating such things are. (My teacher John Gilbert used to call that sort of thing "shoulda-woulda-coulda thinking," and pointed out that it causes vast amounts of useless misery.) Unpacking that programming by way of journaling is a good way to start defusing them.

Second, I recommend using an affirmation to stick a crowbar in the habit of "shoulda-woulda-coulda thinking." Something on the order of "I embrace what is" might be suitable. Repeat it twenty times each morning when you get up. Then, any time you catch yourself comparing something in your life to some "shoulda-woulda-coulda" or other, stop, and repeat the affirmation silently twenty times. That should help you get a handle on those self-defeating fantasies and make it easier for you to revel in the delight of the world as it actually is.

comments.title: Glass, styrofoam and paper cleansing
comments.content:
JMG,

I came across a lovely glass chess set on the sidewalk but my girlfriend gets an uneasy vibe from it. The glass is in firm styrofoam padding within a slightly worn cardboard box. What methods of etheric and astral cleansing would you recommend for these materials (if at all possible)?

Thanks,

Boston

replies.title: Re: Glass, styrofoam and paper cleansing
replies.content:
The glass pieces are easy, because glass doesn't hold magical energies well. Wash them twice, first in hot water with soap, and then in cold water that has had some salt dissolved in it. Rinse each piece thoroughly, and then set them to dry in sunlight. They'll be fine.

Paper and styrofoam are somewhat harder to cleanse, but you can do it. Sprinkle salt, lots of it, all over them. Leave it for an hour or more, and then shake it off into the sink and wash it down with lots of cold water. Let the packing sit in direct sunlight for a few hours. It should be fine.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings folks!

Does anything have a good theory why there’s a slow revival of religion (Christianity mostly but seeing some Islam and Judaism too) going on in the western spheres with intellectuals / intellectual adjacent people like Paul knightsnorth and nick land (who is Christian now as funny as that is).

The whole thing feels slightly hokey and on the nose so I’m trying to understand what’s driving it.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. Oswald Spengler predicted it a century ago in The Decline of the West. When a civilization's Age of Reason winds to a close, as it always does, you get what Spengler called the Second Religiosity -- a revival of old religious forms as a defense against the inner chaos set in motion by the failed Age of Reason. We're seeing that right now. Since scientific rationalism has failed so miserably to bring the shiny Tomorrowland future its publicity agents promised, and scientists have become little more than paid shills for corporate interests, a lot of people are fleeing to older religious forms as an alternative to complete nihilism. You'll see a lot more of that as things proceed.

I didn't know that Nick Land had joined the crowd heading back to Christianity, btw, but it doesn't surprise me at all. I've been predicting for years now that it's precisely the people who make such a big deal about being edgy and antinomian who will be first in the church doors as things proceed. The same thing happened in France at the end of the 19th century; have you ever read J.K. Huysman's Là-Bas, the most famous novel of Satanism in its time? Huysmans ended up as a more than usually devout Catholic...

replies.content:

Yet, these people seem to overreacting to the negative psycho-emotional experience they've gone through, and thus their return to old forms doesn't seem creative/generative, and more defensive, sometimes even aggressive.

Given that we are entering a new age (of aquarius), shouldn't we see new religions or major reformations of the old forms (incl Christianity) with new creative and spiritual energy? What are your thoughts where/when we might see truly new inspired forms of divine ideas?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
I have something rather interesting to add to this: I am in the field of private education. The model I work in is Montessori. I track a lot of the news in education, and the dynamic of the collapse of the Age of Reason and a kind of Second Religiosity is being played out there, too. Conventional public education is collapsing right now on several fronts. For one thing, absenteeism is skyrocketing, up around 25% in many places according to my impression. No one wants to teach in these places anymore, and many teachers that don't leave the profession altogether are setting up mircoschools, homeschool pods etc. Moreover, in reaction to the whole CRT and transgender business, many dissident educators are calling for a return to either Christian classical education in a desks-and-rows format, or to what cells and bells education education was in the 1960s. What that looks like is cells and bells, telling the kids to shut up and sit still in their seats, do the old-fashioned worksheets, and if they don't kick them out of school. Now that system worked after a fashion (especially once you weeded out the genuine troublemakers in addition to kids who simply weren't cut out for it), but it's also the exact system that got us to where we are! That system was dehumanizing, and Leftists (for lack of a better term) took advantage of it to propose their fake "improvements". It's basically a Second Religiosity. I'm not sure if Montessori is a part of that same trend, as it certainly isn't new and is seeing a revival of late. It certainly has a lot of good ideas, but also a few bad ones and some limitations.

What I suspect instead, is that the future dominant models of education are happening on the fringes in microschools, homeschool pods and private schools that are experimenting with mixtures of Montessori, forest school, apprenticeship programs and a host of other ideas to make something new.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
I run a group called Speakeasy Illinois that is/was devoted to promoting freedom of choice establishments. I started it with a co-founder (she is the mother of 5, I am childless by choice) in early 2021. It has about 10K members, a few thousand of whom are active. There were at least four microschools founded by Speakeasy members in 2021-present, and existing microschools saw their ranks swell considerably. There are small religious schools, usually Protestant, plus lots of "learning centers" which are basically home school supplements. One place founded by one of my members in 2021 holds school in a big old barn in the semi-country.

One of my private music students and his father mutually decided upon his exodus from public school and into homeschool right as he turned 12 in 2022. My co-founder's five children were all homeschooled in the early years and are now entering private Catholic school as they hit their teens. For the most part, Catholic schools did not partake in the nonsense of the Covid era. comments.title: Physical Aging Process
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Would you provide a general overview of your view, from a Western Occult perspective, of the physical aging process in humans? Does it have a cause on the subtle, non-material planes? Or, is it a purely physical function that the non-material planes cannot influence?

As an aside, the occult order to which I belong teaches that a person’s use of their sexual energies (and fluids) has a large influence on the aging process—though, to be honest, I find myself rather doubtful of that particular teaching. Do you care to weigh in there?

Thank you!

- balowulf

replies.title: Re: Physical Aging Process
replies.content:
The material plane is the most fragile, brittle, and vulnerable of the planes, and so no material object lasts indefinitely. Those objects we call "bodies," which have the difficult task of providing a material plane vehicle for spirits, are especially vulnerable, and rarely last long; human bodies are unusually long-lived, you know. Aging is simply the process by which such a body runs through its life cycle and breaks down.

Some people, under some conditions, can artificially prolong their lives by redirecting sexual energies. (The fluids are simply vehicles for the energies.) I suppose it would make sense if you were busy with some really prolonged spiritual process, as of course some Asian traditions like to teach. More generally, though, it's a dead end; death and rebirth hit the reset button and help get you out of ruts, and that's an advantage worth having.
